events.title: Emergence of Sumerian City-States
events.detail: The Uruk period (c. 3500–3100 BCE) saw the emergence of the world's first urban civilization in Mesopotamia, with the city of Uruk reaching 20,000–50,000 inhabitants. This period produced the earliest writing system (proto-cuneiform), monumental ziggurat architecture, and complex administrative bureaucracies. Irrigation agriculture along the Tigris and Euphrates enabled surplus production and state-level political organization.

sources.name: Oxford Encyclopedia of Ancient Egypt and Mesopotamia
University of Chicago Oriental Institute Publications

events.title: Invention of Cuneiform Writing at Uruk
events.detail: Sumerian scribes at Uruk developed the world's first writing system around 3200 BCE — cuneiform (Latin: 'wedge-shaped'), pressed into clay tablets with a stylus. Initially used for accounting and administrative records, cuneiform evolved to record mythology, law codes, astronomy, and literature, including the Epic of Gilgamesh — the world's oldest major literary work.

sources.name: British Museum Cuneiform Collection
Journal of the American Oriental Society

events.title: Sargon of Akkad Creates World's First Empire
events.detail: Sargon of Akkad (r. 2334–2279 BCE) conquered the Sumerian city-states and united Mesopotamia under the world's first empire. From his capital Akkad, armies extended control from the Persian Gulf to the Mediterranean. His daughter Enheduanna became the world's first known author by name. The Akkadian Empire lasted ~180 years before collapsing under Gutian invasions.

sources.name: Cambridge Ancient History, Vol. I
Journal of Ancient Near Eastern History

events.title: Third Dynasty of Ur (Ur III) — World's First Bureaucratic State
events.detail: Under Ur-Nammu (r. 2112–2095 BCE) and Shulgi, the Third Dynasty of Ur created the most administratively sophisticated state in the ancient world. Ur-Nammu promulgated the oldest surviving law code in history — predating Hammurabi by 300 years — establishing legal protections including compensation for injury. The Ur III state maintained over 85,000 administrative tablets documenting every aspect of the economy.

sources.name: Penn Museum Ur Excavations Archive
Journal of the American Oriental Society

events.title: Hammurabi Promulgates His Famous Code of Laws
events.detail: Babylon's sixth king, Hammurabi (r. 1792–1750 BCE), unified Mesopotamia and issued his famous legal code circa 1754 BCE. Carved on an 8-foot basalt stele now in the Louvre, the 282 laws covered commercial transactions, property rights, family law, criminal penalties ('an eye for an eye'), and social class distinctions. Under Hammurabi, Babylon became the largest city in the world with an estimated 200,000 inhabitants.

sources.name: Louvre Museum, Code of Hammurabi Stele Documentation
Cambridge Ancient History Volume II

events.title: Assyrian Empire at Its Zenith Under Ashurbanipal
events.detail: Ashurbanipal (r. 668–627 BCE) ruled the largest empire the ancient world had yet seen, stretching from Egypt to Iran. He assembled the first systematically organized library in history at Nineveh — over 30,000 cuneiform tablets including the Standard Babylonian Epic of Gilgamesh. The Assyrian military pioneered siege warfare and cavalry tactics; their mass deportation policies left enduring marks on the region.

sources.name: British Museum, Ashurbanipal Exhibition Catalogue
Oxford Handbook of the Archaeology of the Levant

events.title: Nebuchadnezzar II Rules the Neo-Babylonian Empire
events.detail: Nebuchadnezzar II (r. 605–562 BCE) made Babylon the most splendid city of the ancient world. His building program included the Ishtar Gate (now in Berlin's Pergamon Museum) and the legendary Hanging Gardens. He destroyed Jerusalem in 586 BCE and deported Jewish populations to Babylon. Babylon reached 200,000 population within walls 85 km in circumference.

sources.name: Pergamon Museum, Berlin — Ishtar Gate Documentation
Herodotus, Histories, Book I

events.title: Cyrus the Great Captures Babylon — Persian Rule Begins
events.detail: In October 539 BCE, Achaemenid Persian King Cyrus II captured Babylon, ending Babylonian hegemony. The Cyrus Cylinder — one of the world's first human rights documents — records that Cyrus permitted exiled peoples (including Jews) to return home and restored temples. Mesopotamia became the satrapy of Babylonia within the Achaemenid Empire, lasting until Alexander's conquest in 331 BCE.

sources.name: British Museum, Cyrus Cylinder
Achaemenid Royal Inscriptions Project

era: Persian, Hellenistic, and Sasanian Period (539 BCE–637 CE)

events.title: Alexander the Great Conquers Mesopotamia
events.detail: Following his decisive victory at the Battle of Gaugamela (331 BCE, near modern Mosul), Alexander captured Babylon without resistance and considered making it his imperial capital. He died in Babylon in 323 BCE at age 32. Seleucus subsequently secured Mesopotamia, founding the Seleucid Empire (305 BCE), which ruled until the Parthian conquest in 141 BCE.

sources.name: Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander
Cambridge Companion to Alexander the Great

events.title: Ctesiphon Established as Sasanian Imperial Capital
events.detail: The Sasanian Empire (224–651 CE) made Ctesiphon (near modern Baghdad) its imperial capital. At its height, Ctesiphon housed an estimated 500,000 people — the world's largest city outside China. The Taq Kasra arch — the world's largest single-span brick vault — still stands south of Baghdad. Sasanian bureaucratic, legal, and cultural institutions deeply influenced the subsequent Islamic caliphate.

sources.name: Encyclopedia Iranica — Ctesiphon Entry
Journal of World History, Sasanian Studies

era: Islamic Conquest and Abbasid Golden Age (637–1258)

events.title: Battle of al-Qadisiyya — Arab Muslim Conquest of Iraq
events.detail: In 636 CE, Arab Muslim forces under Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas defeated the Sasanian army at al-Qadisiyya (near modern Kufa). The conquest transformed Iraq: Arabic replaced Aramaic; Islam displaced Zoroastrianism; new cities of Kufa and Basra became centers of early Islamic scholarship. The battle remains a deeply contested symbol — Saddam Hussein named the Iran-Iraq War the 'Qadisiyya of Saddam.'

sources.name: Al-Tabari, The History of the Prophets and Kings
Fred Donner, The Early Islamic Conquests (Princeton, 1981)

events.title: Abbasid Caliph al-Mansur Founds Baghdad
events.detail: In 762 CE, Caliph Abu Ja'far al-Mansur founded Madinat al-Salam ('City of Peace') — Baghdad — as his imperial capital. Built in a perfect circle ~3 km in diameter with the caliph's golden-domed palace at the center, within a century Baghdad had grown to 1–2 million inhabitants, making it the world's largest city and the administrative center of an empire stretching from Spain to Central Asia.

sources.name: Hugh Kennedy, When Baghdad Ruled the Muslim World (Da Capo, 2004)
Al-Khatib al-Baghdadi, History of Baghdad

events.title: House of Wisdom — Baghdad's Golden Age of Science
events.detail: Caliph al-Ma'mun (r. 813–833) fully developed Bayt al-Hikma (House of Wisdom) as the world's premier center of learning. Scholars translated Greek, Persian, Indian, and Syriac texts into Arabic. Original advances in algebra (al-Khwarizmi), optics (Ibn al-Haytham), medicine (Ibn Sina), and astronomy defined the Islamic Golden Age and transmitted knowledge to medieval Europe.

sources.name: Jim Al-Khalili, The House of Wisdom (Penguin, 2011)
George Saliba, Islamic Science and the Making of the European Renaissance (MIT, 2007)

events.title: Hulagu Khan Sacks Baghdad — End of the Abbasid Caliphate
events.detail: In February 1258, Mongol forces under Hulagu Khan besieged and sacked Baghdad, ending the 500-year Abbasid Caliphate. Caliph al-Musta'sim was executed by being wrapped in carpets and trampled. Contemporary accounts describe the Tigris running black with ink from the House of Wisdom's books. Estimates of deaths range from 90,000 to over 800,000. Baghdad's irrigation systems were permanently damaged, transforming regional agriculture for centuries.

sources.name: Peter Jackson, The Mongols and the Islamic World (Yale, 2017)
Ibn Kathir, Al-Bidaya wa'l-Nihaya (The Beginning and the End)

events.title: Suleiman the Magnificent Conquers Baghdad
events.detail: In 1534, Ottoman Sultan Suleiman I captured Baghdad from the Safavid Persians, beginning nearly four centuries of Ottoman rule over Mesopotamia. The 1639 Treaty of Zuhab established boundaries roughly corresponding to the modern Iraq-Iran border. The Ottomans organized the area into three vilayets: Baghdad, Basra, and Mosul. Tanzimat reformer Midhat Pasha (1869–1872) modernized administration, established schools, and laid telegraph cables.

sources.name: Hala Fattah, A Brief History of Iraq (Facts on File, 2008)
Dina Rizk Khoury, State and Provincial Society in the Ottoman Empire (Cambridge, 1997)

events.title: British Mesopotamia Campaign — WWI Conquest
events.detail: British India forces landed at Basra in November 1914 after the Ottomans joined Germany. After the humiliating Siege of Kut (April 1916) where 13,000 British troops surrendered — one of Britain's worst colonial defeats — a reorganized force captured Baghdad in March 1917. By October 1918, Britain controlled all three former Ottoman vilayets, driven primarily by interests in Mesopotamian oil and the route to India.

sources.name: David Fromkin, A Peace to End All Peace (Holt, 1989)
Charles Townshend, When God Made Hell (Faber, 2010)

events.title: Iraqi Revolt of 1920 — Uprising Against British Mandate
events.detail: From May to October 1920, tribes, urban nationalists, Sunni and Shia clergy united in a major anti-British uprising beginning in the middle Euphrates region. British forces used aerial bombardment to suppress the rebellion. Casualties: approximately 10,000 Iraqis and 500 British soldiers killed. The revolt convinced Britain that direct rule was too costly, leading to installation of the Hashemite monarchy. The revolt remains a founding moment of Iraqi nationalist memory.

sources.name: Phebe Marr, The Modern History of Iraq (Westview, 2012)
Hanna Batatu, The Old Social Classes and the Revolutionary Movements of Iraq (Princeton, 1978)

events.title: Iraq Gains Independence — Joins the League of Nations
events.detail: On October 3, 1932, Iraq became the first Arab state to gain full independence, admitted to the League of Nations. The Hashemite King Faisal I (installed by Britain in 1921) navigated the mandate era building Iraqi state institutions. However, structural problems persisted: a British-trained Sunni Arab officer class dominated the military, Shia communities were politically marginalized, and Britain retained treaty rights including air bases. The oil-rich Mosul vilayet was assigned to Iraq in 1926 after major oil discoveries.

sources.name: Toby Dodge, Inventing Iraq (Columbia, 2003)
Hanna Batatu, The Old Social Classes and the Revolutionary Movements of Iraq (Princeton, 1978)

era: Monarchy, Ba'ath Coups, and Saddam's Rise (1932–1979)

events.title: Free Officers' Coup — End of the Hashemite Monarchy
events.detail: On July 14, 1958, Brigadier Abd al-Karim Qasim led a military coup that overthrew the Hashemite monarchy. King Faisal II, Crown Prince Abd al-Ilah, and PM Nuri al-Said were killed. The Republic of Iraq was proclaimed. Qasim pursued neutralist and pan-Arab policies, withdrew from the Baghdad Pact (1959), and nationalized portions of the Iraq Petroleum Company. He was killed in the February 1963 Ba'ath coup.

sources.name: Hanna Batatu, The Old Social Classes and the Revolutionary Movements of Iraq (Princeton, 1978)
US State Department Historical Records — Iraq 1958

events.title: Ba'ath Party Seizes Power — July Revolution
events.detail: On July 17, 1968, the Arab Socialist Ba'ath Party — led by Ahmed Hassan al-Bakr and his cousin Saddam Hussein — staged a bloodless coup and established the Ba'ath Republic. Al-Bakr became president while Saddam Hussein, as head of internal security, consolidated power through the party apparatus. The Ba'ath government nationalized the Iraq Petroleum Company in 1972, channeling oil revenues into development, military buildup, and party patronage networks.

sources.name: Kanan Makiya, Republic of Fear (Berkeley, 1989)
Con Coughlin, Saddam: His Rise and Fall (Harper, 2002)

events.title: Iraq Nationalizes the Iraq Petroleum Company
events.detail: On June 1, 1972, the Ba'ath government nationalized the Iraq Petroleum Company (IPC), which had been majority-owned by British, French, and American oil companies since the 1920s. The 1973 oil crisis then quadrupled prices, delivering a massive windfall: Iraq's GDP grew from $3.4 billion (1972) to $21.5 billion (1979). This wealth funded infrastructure, schools, hospitals, and a formidable military — but also entrenched Ba'ath patronage networks.

sources.name: OPEC Annual Report 1975
Joe Stork, Middle East Oil and the Energy Crisis (Monthly Review, 1975)

events.title: Saddam Hussein Assumes Presidency — Ba'ath Party Purge
events.detail: On July 16, 1979, Saddam Hussein assumed the presidency after forcing ailing President al-Bakr to resign. Within days, he convened a televised senior Ba'ath Party meeting, reading out a list of 68 'traitors' accused of a pro-Syrian coup plot. 22 were executed by firing squads of their former colleagues, establishing a culture of collective guilt. Saddam consolidated total control over state, military, party, and security services, building a Stalinist-style cult of personality.

sources.name: Kanan Makiya, Republic of Fear (Berkeley, 1989)
Human Rights Watch, Iraq Country Reports 1979-1989

era: Iran-Iraq War, Halabja, and Gulf War (1980–1991)

events.title: Iran-Iraq War Begins — Saddam Invades Iran
events.detail: On September 22, 1980, Iraqi forces launched a full-scale invasion of Iran, beginning an 8-year war costing over one million lives. Saddam sought to seize oil-rich Khuzestan, reverse the 1975 Algiers Accord on the Shatt al-Arab waterway, and contain the Iranian Islamic Revolution. The war quickly became trench warfare reminiscent of WWI, with mass infantry assaults, chemical weapons, and tanker wars in the Persian Gulf. The US and Gulf Arab states provided Iraq financial and intelligence support.

sources.name: Shahram Chubin and Charles Tripp, Iran and Iraq at War (Westview, 1988)
SIPRI Arms Transfers Database, Iran-Iraq War

events.title: Halabja Chemical Attack — Anfal Campaign Against Kurds
events.detail: On March 16-17, 1988, Iraqi aircraft dropped chemical weapons — mustard gas and nerve agents — on the Kurdish town of Halabja during the Anfal campaign. Between 3,200 and 5,000 civilians were killed, making it the largest chemical weapons attack against a civilian population in history. The broader Anfal campaign (1986-1989) killed an estimated 100,000–182,000 Kurds and destroyed over 4,000 villages. In 2005, the Iraqi High Tribunal ruled the Anfal campaign a genocide.

sources.name: Human Rights Watch, Genocide in Iraq: The Anfal Campaign Against the Kurds (1993)
Iraqi High Tribunal Ruling on Anfal, June 2007
CIA Intelligence Memorandum on Iraqi Chemical Weapons Use, 1988

events.title: Iraq Invades Kuwait — UN Imposes Comprehensive Sanctions
events.detail: On August 2, 1990, Iraq invaded Kuwait with 100,000 troops, occupying the country in 12 hours. Saddam claimed Kuwait was historically part of Iraq and accused it of stealing Iraqi oil via slant-drilling in the Rumaila field. The UN Security Council imposed comprehensive sanctions (Resolution 661) and authorized force if Iraq did not withdraw. A 34-nation coalition led by the United States began forming.

sources.name: UN Security Council Resolutions 660, 661 (1990)
Rick Atkinson, Crusade: The Untold Story of the Persian Gulf War (Houghton Mifflin, 1993)

events.title: Operation Desert Storm — US-Led Coalition Liberates Kuwait
events.detail: On January 17, 1991, the US-led coalition began a 39-day air campaign. The ground war (February 24-28) lasted 100 hours and expelled Iraqi forces from Kuwait. Coalition casualties: ~292 killed; Iraqi military casualties: 20,000–35,000 killed. Saddam's forces set fire to 700 Kuwaiti oil wells before retreating. The ceasefire left Saddam in power — a decision later heavily debated. Post-war UN sanctions continued until 2003, devastating the Iraqi civilian economy.

sources.name: Pentagon, Operation Desert Storm After-Action Report (1992)
UNSCR 687 (1991) — Ceasefire and Disarmament Terms

events.title: UN Oil-for-Food Programme Established
events.detail: After years of devastating sanctions, the UN established the Oil-for-Food Programme in 1996. Iraq could sell limited oil with revenues restricted to food, medicine, and humanitarian imports. By 2003, $64 billion in oil had been sold. A 2004 UN investigation (the 'Volcker Report') documented that Saddam's government extracted $1.8 billion in illegal surcharges from 2,200 companies in 66 countries, while the program nevertheless prevented a worse humanitarian crisis in a country where infant mortality had doubled since 1991.

sources.name: Independent Inquiry Committee into the UN Oil-for-Food Programme (Volcker Report, 2004)
UNICEF, Child and Maternal Mortality Survey, Iraq 1999

events.title: US-Led Invasion — Operation Iraqi Freedom Begins
events.detail: On March 20, 2003, US-led coalition forces launched Operation Iraqi Freedom, citing alleged Iraqi WMDs and al-Qaeda links — both claims later found false or heavily exaggerated. Baghdad fell April 9; Saddam's statue was toppled in Firdos Square. Saddam was captured December 13, 2003 near Tikrit, tried by the Iraqi Special Tribunal, and executed December 30, 2006. The invasion was conducted without UN Security Council authorization, creating lasting legal controversy.

sources.name: Pentagon, Operation Iraqi Freedom After-Action Report (2003)
Chilcot Inquiry, Report of the Iraq Inquiry (UK, 2016)
UN Secretary-General Kofi Annan Statement on Iraq War Legality, 2004

events.title: CPA Orders De-Ba'athification and Dissolution of Iraqi Army
events.detail: Coalition Provisional Authority Administrator Paul Bremer issued CPA Order No. 1 (May 16, 2003) banning top Ba'ath members from government, purging 30,000–50,000 mid-level technocrats. CPA Order No. 2 (May 23) dissolved the Iraqi military, instantly creating 400,000 unemployed, armed former soldiers without pay. Widely condemned by US military commanders and the Baker-Hamilton Iraq Study Group, these decisions fueled the Sunni insurgency and provided recruits for al-Qaeda in Iraq and eventually ISIS.

sources.name: CPA Orders 1 and 2, Coalition Provisional Authority (2003)
Iraq Study Group Report (Baker-Hamilton Commission, 2006)

events.title: Samarra Mosque Bombing Triggers Sectarian Civil War
events.detail: On February 22, 2006, bombs destroyed the golden dome of the Al-Askari Mosque in Samarra — one of Shia Islam's holiest shrines. Al-Qaeda in Iraq (AQI) led by Abu Musab al-Zarqawi claimed responsibility. The attack triggered an explosion of sectarian violence: 30-40 unidentified bodies per day appeared in Baghdad streets. By 2007, Iraq was in full sectarian civil war with 100,000+ excess deaths. UNHCR estimated 2.2 million Iraqis fled the country and 2.7 million were internally displaced.

sources.name: UNHCR Iraq Situation Report 2007
Iraq Body Count, Documented Civilian Deaths 2003-2008

events.title: US Troop Surge — Violence Declines Dramatically
events.detail: President Bush announced the 'Surge' in January 2007 — deploying 20,000 additional troops (peak: 170,000) and shifting to counterinsurgency strategy under General David Petraeus. New tactics included the 'Awakening Movement' (Sahwa) — paying Sunni tribal leaders to fight AQI. Violence fell from ~3,000 civilian deaths/month (2006) to ~500/month by 2008. However, political reconciliation under PM Nouri al-Maliki's sectarian governance was never achieved.

sources.name: General David Petraeus, Multi-National Force Iraq Report to Congress (2007)
RAND, Counterinsurgency in Iraq 2003-2009 (2010)

events.title: US Combat Forces Withdraw from Iraq
events.detail: On December 18, 2011, the last US combat troops left Iraq, ending a nearly 9-year occupation mandated by the 2008 US-Iraq Status of Forces Agreement. The US had lost 4,431 military personnel; coalition partners, 316. Total cost to the US: approximately $2 trillion, rising to $6 trillion with long-term veteran care (Brown University Costs of War Project). The Iraqi military, US-trained and equipped, proved dangerously unprepared for the ISIS threat to come.

sources.name: US Department of Defense, OIF Summary Statistics (2011)
Brown University Costs of War Project, Iraq War Spending Estimates

events.title: ISIS Captures Mosul — Declares Islamic Caliphate
events.detail: On June 10, 2014, approximately 1,500 ISIS fighters overran Mosul — Iraq's second-largest city of 1.8 million — after four Iraqi Army divisions (nominally 30,000 soldiers) fled. Within weeks, ISIS controlled ~40% of Iraq's territory. On June 29, ISIS leader Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi declared a 'Caliphate' from the Mosul Grand Mosque. The US began airstrikes in August 2014 and formed a 60-nation coalition. PM Maliki resigned, replaced by Haider al-Abadi.

sources.name: CENTCOM, Operation Inherent Resolve Campaign Reports (2014)
International Crisis Group, Iraq: Fallout from the ISIS Offensive (2014)
Reuters, ISIS Captures Mosul (June 2014)

events.title: ISIS Yazidi Genocide — Sinjar Massacre
events.detail: On August 3, 2014, ISIS launched a genocidal attack on the Yazidi religious minority in Sinjar. Men and boys were executed en masse; women and children were enslaved and sold (ISIS published price lists). The UN, US Congress, and EU Parliament formally recognized the attacks as genocide. An estimated 5,000–10,000 Yazidis were killed and 6,000–7,000 women and children enslaved. 40,000+ Yazidis trapped on Mount Sinjar prompted expanded US military intervention.

sources.name: OHCHR, They Came to Destroy: ISIS Crimes Against the Yazidis (2016)
Human Rights Watch, Iraq: ISIS Escapees Describe Systematic Rape (2015)

events.title: Battle of Mosul — Largest Urban Battle of the 21st Century
events.detail: The Battle of Mosul (October 2016 – July 2017) involved over 100,000 fighters — Iraqi Security Forces, Kurdish Peshmerga, Popular Mobilization Forces, and Coalition Special Operations. ISIS had fortified the city with thousands of VBIEDs, tunnel networks, and human shields. The battle lasted 9 months. The UN estimated 800,000 civilians displaced; ~10,000 killed. On July 9, 2017, PM al-Abadi declared victory. The Grand al-Nuri Mosque was blown up by ISIS on June 21, 2017.

sources.name: UNAMI, Human Rights Report: Battle for Mosul (2017)
CENTCOM, Operation Inherent Resolve Campaign Update (July 2017)
Amnesty International, At Any Cost: The Civilian Catastrophe in West Mosul (2017)

events.title: ISIS Territorial Defeat Declared in Iraq
events.detail: On December 9, 2017, Iraqi PM Haider al-Abadi declared final victory over ISIS, ending its self-declared 'Caliphate' territory in Iraq. At its peak (2014), ISIS controlled ~110,000 km² and 8 million people. The military victory came at massive cost: an estimated $45–100 billion in infrastructure damage (World Bank), 3.4 million Iraqis displaced, and thousands of documented coalition airstrike civilian casualties. ISIS shifted to insurgency and continued bombings and assassinations through 2025.

sources.name: Iraqi Prime Minister's Office Statement, December 9, 2017
World Bank, Iraq Damage Assessment Report (2018)

events.title: October Revolution — Mass Protests Against Corrupt Governance
events.detail: Beginning October 1, 2019, Iraq experienced its largest protests since 2003 — the 'Tishreen Revolution' — with millions demanding an end to corruption, sectarian governance, Iranian interference, and unemployment. Security forces and Iranian-backed PMF units killed over 600 protesters and wounded 25,000, using live fire and high-velocity tear gas rounds. PM Adil Abdul-Mahdi resigned in November 2019. The protests were the first major cross-sectarian youth uprising against the post-2003 political system.

sources.name: UNAMI Report on October Protests, 2019-2020
Human Rights Watch, Iraq: Security Forces Kill Protesters (2019)
Al Jazeera, Iraq Tishreen Protests Coverage (2019)

events.title: US Kills Qasem Soleimani at Baghdad Airport
events.detail: On January 3, 2020, a US drone strike at Baghdad International Airport killed Iranian IRGC Quds Force commander Qasem Soleimani and Abu Mahdi al-Muhandis, deputy head of Iraq's Popular Mobilization Forces. Iran retaliated January 8 by launching 15 ballistic missiles at two US-Iraq bases (Ain al-Assad and Erbil), injuring 110 US troops. The Iraqi parliament voted to expel US forces; the US refused to leave, making Iraq the primary US-Iran proxy battlefield.

sources.name: US Department of Defense Statement on Soleimani Strike (January 3, 2020)
Reuters, Soleimani Killing and Aftermath (January 2020)

events.title: Mohammed Shia al-Sudani Becomes Prime Minister
events.detail: On October 27, 2022, Mohammed Shia al-Sudani was sworn in as Iraq's Prime Minister, ending a 13-month post-election deadlock. Backed by the Iran-aligned Coordination Framework (including Nouri al-Maliki's State of Law and PMF-affiliated parties), al-Sudani took office after Muqtada al-Sadr's bloc withdrew following a Green Zone standoff. Al-Sudani has balanced ties with Iran while courting US and Gulf Arab investment, negotiating disputed Kirkuk oil revenues with the KRG, and promising anti-corruption reforms.

sources.name: Iraqi Council of Representatives, PM Confirmation Vote, October 2022
Reuters, Iraq's Sudani Sworn in as Prime Minister (2022)
International Crisis Group, Iraq: Avoiding a Return to Political Crisis (2023)

events.title: Iraq and US Announce Transition of Military Presence
events.detail: In September 2024, Iraq and the US announced a framework for transitioning the US military presence — reducing approximately 2,500 US troops from a combat role by end of 2025, with a smaller advisory mission continuing. The deal followed months of negotiations prompted by PMF rocket attacks on US bases and fallout from the Soleimani killing. The agreement shifts to a bilateral security partnership focused on counterterrorism intelligence and training, while critics warn ISIS retains an active insurgency.

sources.name: US State Department Statement on Iraq Security Partnership (September 2024)
Iraqi PM al-Sudani Joint Statement (September 2024)
RAND Corporation, US Military Presence in Iraq: Transition Risks (2024)
